cards.text: A range-based for loop (for-each) has the syntax: {{c1::for (element_declaration : container)}}

cards.question: Write a range-based for loop to print all elements
cards.explanation: Range-based for loop iterates through each element automatically.

cards.text: Use a range-based for loop when you need to access each element {{c1::sequentially}} and {{c2::don't need the index}}. They're simpler, more readable, and less {{c3::error-prone}}.
To modify elements in a range-based for loop, use a {{c1::reference}}: for ({{c2::int&}} val : vec)

cards.question: What does this code do?
cards.explanation: The range-based for loop with reference (int&) modifies each element in place, doubling values. v[0] becomes 2.

cards.text: To read elements without copying and prevent modification, use {{c1::const auto&}}: for ({{c1::const auto&}} val : vec)

cards.concept: Choosing the element type in range-based for loops
cards.explanation:
Three common patterns:
  • int val : vec → copies each element (OK for cheap types)
  • int& val : vec → reference to modify elements
  • const auto& val : vec → const reference (best for read-only)
Use const auto& as default for read-only to avoid copies and work with any type.
examples: for (const auto& val : vec)  // Best for read-only
for (auto& val : vec)  // To modify elements
for (int val : vec)  // OK for int, but copies

cards.text: const auto& is preferred in range-based for loops because it {{c1::avoids copies}}, works with {{c2::any element type}}, and prevents {{c3::accidental modification}}.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Outputs 1 (unchanged). val is a copy, so modifying it doesn't affect the vector.
cards.explanation: Need to use int& val to modify the actual elements in the vector.

cards.front: What are the main limitations of range-based for loops?
cards.back:
• Cannot access the element index directly
  • Always iterates forward through all elements
  • Cannot skip elements or iterate backwards
  • Cannot modify the container size during iteration

cards.text: Range-based for loops were introduced in {{c1::C++11}} and are sometimes called {{c2::for-each}} loops.

cards.question: What type is val in this loop?
cards.explanation: auto deduces the element type (std::string), and const& makes it a const reference.
